_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against ILAE 2017 Classification of Epilepsy, NICE CG137 Epilepsy, WHO Epilepsy Report, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Epilepsy: Types, Causes, Diagnosis, Seizure Management, and Treatment
heroOverview: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder defined by a sustained predisposition to generate unprovoked epileptic seizures, characterized by abnormal, excessive, or hypersynchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It is one of the world's oldest recognized medical conditions, yet remains the world's most common serious neurological disorder, affecting approximately 55 million people globally. The diagnosis requires at least two unprovoked seizures occurring more than 24 hours apart, or one unprovoked seizure with a probability of further seizures equivalent to the recurrence risk after two unprovoked seizures. Epilepsy is not a single disease but a spectrum of disorders with diverse causes, seizure types, and outcomes ranging from benign, self-limited syndromes to progressive, treatment-resistant conditions that profoundly impact cognition, safety, and quality of life.

keyStats.prevalence: 55 million people globally (WHO 2023); 1 in 26 people will develop epilepsy in their lifetime
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal distribution — peak in early childhood (under 5 years) and in adults over 60; incidence higher in low- and middle-income countries
keyStats.avgAge: Median onset varies by syndrome: childhood absence epilepsy (4-8 years), juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (12-18 years), focal epilepsy in elderly (over 65)
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 50 million people with active epilepsy requiring ongoing treatment (WHO 2023); 80% live in low- and middle-income countries

definition: Epilepsy (ICD-10: G40 for Epilepsy; G41 for Status Epilepticus) is diagnosed when a person has at least two unprovoked seizures occurring at least 24 hours apart, or one unprovoked seizure with a probability of recurrence estimated to be at least 60% over the next 10 years. A small proportion of epilepsies are reflex epilepsies, triggered by specific sensory stimuli (flashing lights, patterns, sounds) or cognitive/emotional triggers. Epilepsy is a disorder of brain networks — seizures result from excessive, hypersynchronous firing of neurons in cortical or subcortical structures. The International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) 2017 operational classification separates epilepsies by seizure type (focal onset, generalized onset, unknown onset), by epilepsy type (focal, generalized, combined generalized and focal, unknown), and by epilepsy syndrome. Causes are stratified into structural (lesions visible on MRI), genetic, infectious, metabolic, and immune.

typesClassification.description: Seizures originating within networks limited to one hemisphere, which may secondarily generalize. Focal aware seizures (previously simple partial) occur without impaired awareness; focal impaired awareness seizures (previously complex partial) involve altered consciousness. Automatisms (lip-smacking, fumbling), tonic or clonic motor activity, sensory symptoms, and autonomic symptoms are common manifestations depending on the cortical region involved.

Seizures originating within and rapidly engaging bilateral distributed networks. Includes tonic-clonic seizures (loss of consciousness, stiffening then rhythmic jerking), absence seizures (brief loss of awareness lasting 5-30 seconds), myoclonic seizures (sudden brief jerks), atonic seizures (sudden loss of tone causing drops), and clonic seizures.

When the onset of a seizure cannot be determined as focal or generalized, typically early in the diagnostic process or when EEG and imaging data are unavailable. Classification may evolve as more information becomes available.

An epilepsy syndrome with onset between 4-10 years, characterized by frequent (up to hundreds per day) absence seizures presenting as brief staring spells with immediate return to full alertness. Associated with 3 Hz spike-and-wave on EEG. Two-thirds of patients have an excellent prognosis with appropriate medication.

An idiopathic generalized epilepsy syndrome with onset in adolescence (12-18 years), characterized by myoclonic jerks on awakening, generalized tonic-clonic seizures, and often absence seizures. Strongly genetic. Lifelong medication is typically required; carbamazepine can worsen myoclonic seizures.

A seizure type in which a focal-onset seizure spreads to engage both hemispheres, producing a secondary generalized tonic-clonic seizure. The focal onset may be clinically apparent (a preceding aura) or only evident on EEG.

A life-threatening neurological emergency defined as continuous seizure activity lasting more than 5 minutes, or two or more seizures without recovery between them. Convulsive status epilepticus is a medical emergency requiring immediate benzodiazepine treatment.

primarySymptoms: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure: sudden loss of consciousness with whole-body stiffening (tonic phase, 10-20 seconds) followed by rhythmic jerking (clonic phase, 1-2 minutes), often with tongue biting, urinary incontinence, and postictal confusion.
Focal impaired awareness seizure: altered consciousness with staring, automatisms (lip-smacking, fumbling), confused behavior, and postictal disorientation lasting 1-2 minutes.
Absence seizure: brief, sudden loss of awareness lasting 5-30 seconds, typically in children, with immediate return to normal function.
Myoclonic seizure: sudden, brief, shock-like jerk of a muscle group lasting less than a second. May cause the person to drop objects or fall.
Atonic seizure (drop attack): sudden loss of muscle tone causing the head to drop, knees to buckle, or complete collapse. High risk of injury.

Focal aware seizure (aura): a seizure beginning in a specific brain region producing symptoms localizable to that area — visual disturbances, auditory phenomena, olfactory hallucinations, epigastric rising sensation, or unilateral tingling.

Postictal state: the period following a seizure characterized by confusion, disorientation, drowsiness, headache, and sometimes focal neurological deficits (Todd's paralysis) lasting minutes to hours.
Prodrome: hours to days before a seizure, some patients experience mood changes (irritability, depression), sleep disturbances, or autonomic changes.
earlyWarningSigns: Brief staring spells that are new in a child, particularly if frequent and the child has no memory of them afterward
Sudden, unexplained myoclonic jerks or drop attacks without preceding trauma
Episodes of automatisms (repetitive lip-smacking, fumbling) with altered awareness — may be mistaken for daydreaming or behavioral issues
New-onset seizures in an adult, particularly focal seizures — requires urgent investigation for underlying causes (brain tumor, stroke, infection)
Increasing frequency of seizures despite antiepileptic medication — a sign that the treatment regimen may need adjustment
Sleep-related generalized tonic-clonic seizures — a hallmark of generalized genetic epilepsies
emergencySigns: Status epilepticus — a seizure lasting more than 5 minutes, or multiple seizures without full recovery between them, is a life-threatening emergency
First-ever seizure in an adult requires emergency evaluation to exclude serious underlying causes including stroke, brain hemorrhage, tumor, or infection
Seizure with sustained fever, neck stiffness, or severe headache suggests meningitis or encephalitis
Seizure accompanied by focal neurological deficits that persist beyond the postictal period suggests acute stroke
Severe head trauma during a seizure suggesting intracranial injury

causes.description: MRI-visible abnormalities that lower the seizure threshold. Includes hippocampal sclerosis (the most common surgical pathology in temporal lobe epilepsy), cortical dysplasia (a leading cause of pediatric drug-resistant epilepsy), brain tumors, vascular malformations, stroke, traumatic brain injury, and encephalomalacia from prior infections or surgery.

Epilepsies caused by inherited or de novo genetic mutations without a separate structural lesion. Includes Dravet syndrome (SCN1A mutation), tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC1/TSC2), Rett syndrome (MECP2), and many monogenic epilepsies. Polygenic risk contributes to common generalized epilepsies like JME and childhood absence epilepsy.

causes.cause: Infections of the central nervous system
causes.description: CNS infections are the most common cause of epilepsy globally. Neurocysticercosis (parasitic infection, the leading cause of epilepsy worldwide in endemic areas), meningitis, encephalitis (herpes simplex encephalitis carries high seizure risk), HIV/AIDS, cerebral malaria, and post-infectious autoimmune encephalitis can all cause epilepsy.

Moderate-to-severe TBI carries a 2-5% risk of developing post-traumatic epilepsy, with risk increasing to 15-20% for penetrating head injuries or those with intracranial hemorrhage. Risk persists for years after injury.
Stroke is the leading cause of epilepsy in older adults. Ischemic stroke, particularly when involving the cortex, carries a 3-10% risk of post-stroke epilepsy; hemorrhagic stroke carries higher risk.

Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-LGI1, anti-NMDA receptor, anti-GAD65), CNS vasculitis, Rasmussen encephalitis, and neuroinflammatory conditions cause epilepsy through antibody-mediated neuronal dysfunction. Seizures in these conditions often require immunotherapy.

Electrolyte disturbances (severe hyponatremia, hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia), hypoglycemia, uremia, hepatic encephalopathy, and inborn errors of metabolism can provoke acute symptomatic seizures. These are considered provoked seizures and do not constitute epilepsy unless they recur without the metabolic trigger.

riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of people with epilepsy have a 2-4 fold increased risk. The risk is highest for generalized epilepsy syndromes and lowest for focal epilepsies from structural causes.
Moderate and severe TBI, particularly with cortical involvement, intracranial hemorrhage, or early post-traumatic seizures, significantly increases epilepsy risk.
Cerebral infarction, hemorrhage, and vascular malformations are major causes of epilepsy in adults over 60. Every stroke increases epilepsy risk by approximately 20-fold in the acute period.
History of meningitis, encephalitis, or neurocysticercosis dramatically increases epilepsy risk. This is the dominant cause of epilepsy globally in endemic regions.
Alzheimer's disease, frontotemporal dementia, and other neurodegenerative conditions carry significantly elevated epilepsy risk, with 10-20% of AD patients experiencing seizures.
Complex febrile seizures (lasting more than 15 minutes, focal, or recurrent within 24 hours) increase the risk of subsequent epilepsy from 1% to 5-10%.
Sleep deprivation lowers the seizure threshold and is a common trigger for seizures in people with epilepsy. Adequate sleep hygiene is a foundational self-management strategy.
Excessive alcohol consumption and, particularly, alcohol withdrawal (which causes cortical hyperexcitability) are significant seizure triggers.

Approximately 3% of people with epilepsy are photosensitive — their seizures are triggered by flickering lights or specific visual patterns. Photosensitivity is most common in children and adolescents with generalized epilepsy.
Missing doses of antiepileptic medication is one of the most common causes of breakthrough seizures. Medication adherence is critical for seizure control.

affectedDemographics: Epilepsy affects approximately 55 million people worldwide — more than multiple sclerosis, cerebral palsy, and Parkinson's disease combined
Incidence is highest in early childhood and in adults over 60
In low- and middle-income countries, the incidence of epilepsy is nearly twice that of high-income countries
Approximately 30% of people with epilepsy have drug-resistant epilepsy
People with epilepsy have a 2-3 fold increased mortality risk compared to the general population
Stigma and discrimination against people with epilepsy remain significant globally
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of epilepsy requires a detailed clinical history — ideally from both the patient and a witness — to characterize the events, and differentiation from mimics (syncope, TIA, migraine, psychogenic non-epileptic seizures, parasomnias, movement disorders). Video-EEG monitoring is the cornerstone of diagnosis and classification, capturing the ictal EEG pattern to distinguish focal from generalized onset. MRI of the brain with epilepsy protocol is mandatory in all new-onset epilepsy to identify structural causes. Laboratory testing excludes provoked seizures. The distinction between epilepsy and a single unprovoked seizure is important — the former requires long-term treatment and carries recurrence risk of approximately 60-70% over 10 years.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Records electrical brain activity to identify interictal epileptiform discharges and characterize ictal onset patterns. Video-EEG captures the clinical event simultaneously with EEG, allowing classification of seizure type and localization of onset.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine EEG takes 20-40 minutes. Video-EEG monitoring requires hospitalization for 1-5 days to capture habitual seizures.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural causes of epilepsy including hippocampal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, tumors, vascular malformations, and gliotic scars.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 45-minute MRI scan using high-resolution thin-slice sequences optimized for the hippocampus and cortex.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests — metabolic panel, liver function, drug levels
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes metabolic causes of seizures, assesses organ function relevant to antiepileptic drug metabolism, and monitors drug levels when toxicity is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Extends EEG recording to 24-72 hours outside the hospital setting, increasing the likelihood of capturing interictal epileptiform activity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A portable EEG device attached to scalp electrodes worn for 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Functional imaging showing interictal hypometabolism in the seizure onset zone. Particularly useful in temporal lobe epilepsy when MRI is inconclusive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Injectable radioactive tracer with imaging. Does not require hospitalization.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of epilepsy is individualized based on seizure type, epilepsy syndrome, EEG findings, MRI results, age, comorbidities, and patient preferences. Antiseizure medications (ASMs) are the primary treatment — over 25 are available, and selection is driven by seizure type. For focal epilepsy, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, and carbamazepine are common first-line choices. For generalized epilepsies, valproate, levetiracetam, and lamotrigine are preferred; carbamazepine and phenytoin can worsen generalized seizures. Approximately 60-70% of people achieve seizure freedom with the first or second ASM. For drug-resistant epilepsy, evaluation for epilepsy surgery, neurostimulation devices (VNS, RNS, DBS), or dietary therapies (ketogenic diet) is warranted.

medicalTreatments.name: Lamotrigine 25-500 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: A broad-spectrum ASM effective for both focal and generalized seizures. Often first-line due to favorable tolerability. Requires slow titration over 8 weeks to reduce the risk of serious rash (Stevens-Johnson syndrome). Drug interactions: valproate increases lamotrigine levels significantly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seizure freedom in approximately 50-60% of patients as first monotherapy for focal epilepsy.

medicalTreatments.name: Levetiracetam 500-3000 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: A broad-spectrum ASM with a unique mechanism (SV2A binding) and few drug interactions. Side effects include behavioral effects (irritability, depression in 10-15%), somnolence, and dizziness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent efficacy to carbamazepine for focal epilepsy. Highly flexible dosing.

medicalTreatments.name: Valproate 500-2500 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: A broad-spectrum ASM effective for virtually all seizure types, including absence, myoclonic, and generalized tonic-clonic. In women of childbearing potential: use only when no effective alternative exists due to teratogenicity (neural tube defects in 1-2% of exposed pregnancies).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: High efficacy across seizure types. Teratogenicity and metabolic side effects limit use in women of reproductive age.

medicalTreatments.name: Carbamazepine 200-1600 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Effective for focal seizures but can worsen generalized and absence seizures — a critical contraindication. Requires slow titration, therapeutic drug monitoring, and monitoring for hyponatremia and rare hematological reactions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Established efficacy for focal epilepsy; NNT for seizure freedom approximately 4.

medicalTreatments.name: Brivaracetam 50-200 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: A newer ASM with higher affinity for SV2A than levetiracetam. Similar broad-spectrum efficacy with potentially fewer behavioral side effects. Minimal drug interactions and no required titration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to levetiracetam in focal epilepsy trials; approved as adjunctive and monotherapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Lacosamide 200-600 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: An ASM with a novel mechanism (enhancing sodium channel slow inactivation) providing add-on efficacy for focal seizures. Good tolerability with minimal cognitive effects. Can be given intravenously for status epilepticus.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 30-40% responder rates as add-on therapy; approved as monotherapy and adjunctive for focal epilepsy.

medicalTreatments.name: Ethosuximide 500-1500 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for childhood absence epilepsy (absence seizures only). Highly effective for absence seizures with a favorable tolerability profile. Does NOT protect against generalized tonic-clonic or myoclonic seizures.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Absence seizure freedom in 53-70% of patients with childhood absence epilepsy.

surgicalOptions.name: Anterior temporal lobe resection
surgicalOptions.description: The most common and most successful epilepsy surgery — removal of the anterior 2-3 cm of the temporal lobe including the amygdala and hippocampus, for mesial temporal sclerosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 60-80% achieve seizure freedom at 5 years; the highest success rate of any epilepsy surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Vagus Nerve Stimulation (VNS)
surgicalOptions.description: A neurostimulation device implanted in the chest with electrodes wrapped around the left vagus nerve, delivering regular electrical pulses. Reduces seizure frequency and severity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Median seizure reduction of 50-60% at 5 years; approximately 5-10% of patients achieve seizure freedom.

surgicalOptions.name: Responsive Neurostimulation (RNS System)
surgicalOptions.description: An implanted device that detects seizures via intracranial electrodes and delivers targeted electrical stimulation to the seizure focus to abort seizures in real time.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 30% seizure freedom at 5 years, with 75% experiencing at least 50% seizure reduction.

surgicalOptions.name: Corpus callosotomy
surgicalOptions.description: A palliative procedure in which the fibers connecting the two cerebral hemispheres are cut, preventing the spread of seizure activity from one hemisphere to the other. Reduces generalized tonic-clonic and atonic (drop) seizures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant reduction in atonic seizures (drop attacks) in 60-80% of patients. Not curative but can substantially improve safety and quality of life.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ketogenic Diet
alternativeTreatments.description: A high-fat, low-carbohydrate diet that induces ketosis, a metabolic state that reduces neuronal excitability. Primarily used in children with drug-resistant epilepsy. Requires strict adherence and supervision by a dietitian.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 30-50% achieve 50% or greater seizure reduction; 10-15% achieve seizure freedom. Most effective in children with glucose transporter type 1 deficiency.

alternativeTreatments.name: Modified Atkins Diet
alternativeTreatments.description: A less restrictive alternative to the ketogenic diet, allowing more protein and vegetables while maintaining severe carbohydrate restriction. Emerging evidence for similar efficacy in adults.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 30-40% achieve 50% or greater seizure reduction in adults.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological interventions — stress reduction and seizure first aid training
alternativeTreatments.description: Mindfulness-based stress reduction, CBT, and seizure first aid training can reduce seizure-related anxiety and improve safety. Seizure alert devices provide early warning of tonic-clonic seizures.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunctive benefit in reducing seizure-related disability and improving quality of life.

whySeeSpecialist: A neurologist, particularly one with epilepsy subspecialty training (an epileptologist), should manage epilepsy because accurate diagnosis and classification directly determine medication choice, and many non-specialists prescribe incorrectly. Referral is indicated for: all new-onset seizures in adults; drug-resistant epilepsy; first seizure with focal features on EEG or MRI abnormalities; epilepsy in women of childbearing age; status epilepticus; and any patient with frequent seizures or significant medication side effects.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a neurologist with epilepsy subspecialty certification or fellowship training in epilepsy. Academic epilepsy centers offer video-EEG monitoring, epilepsy surgery evaluation, dietary therapy programs, and clinical trials. Ask about the center's surgical volume for epilepsy surgery — higher volume correlates with better outcomes. For women with epilepsy, seek a specialist comfortable with pre-conception counseling and ASM management in pregnancy.
preventionStrategies: Prevent traumatic brain injury: wear seatbelts, helmets for cycling and motorcycling, fall prevention in elderly
Treat CNS infections promptly: meningitis, encephalitis, and neurocysticercosis all increase epilepsy risk if not treated early
Stroke prevention: manage hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and cardiovascular risk factors
Prenatal care: prevent perinatal injury through adequate obstetric care
Medication adherence: take antiseizure medications exactly as prescribed
Avoid seizure triggers: sleep deprivation, excessive alcohol, and flashing lights for photosensitive patients
lifestyleModifications: Sleep hygiene: aim for 7-9 hours of consistent sleep nightly; sleep deprivation is a potent seizure trigger
Alcohol in moderation or abstention: avoid binge drinking and drinking to the point of intoxication
Driving regulations: comply with local licensing regulations — most require seizure freedom of 6-12 months before driving
Shower safety: use walk-in showers rather than bathtubs; avoid locking bathroom doors; consider a buddy system
Swimming precautions: never swim alone; wear a life jacket near water; inform lifeguards of seizure tendency
Workplace safety: discuss workplace accommodations with your employer; avoid working at heights or with unguarded machinery if seizures are not fully controlled
Seizure diary: track seizure frequency, timing, potential triggers, and medication side effects

recommended: Ketogenic diet (under neurologist and dietitian supervision): for drug-resistant epilepsy, particularly in children
Consistent meal timing: regular meals prevent hypoglycemia, which can trigger seizures in some patients
Hydration: adequate water intake; some ASMs (topiramate, zonisamide) increase risk of kidney stones
Folate supplementation: critical for women with epilepsy on enzyme-inducing ASMs and for all women planning pregnancy
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, leafy greens, whole grains): some evidence for modest seizure threshold benefits
avoid: Alcohol excess: alcohol lowers seizure threshold acutely and causes withdrawal-related seizures
Excessive caffeine: high doses can trigger seizures in some patients; moderate consumption is generally safe
Very low carbohydrate diets (except under medical supervision): can cause electrolyte shifts that may lower seizure threshold
Grapefruit juice: inhibits CYP3A4 and can increase levels of carbamazepine and other ASMs

exerciseGuidelines: Regular physical exercise is beneficial and safe for most people with epilepsy — it improves cardiovascular health, reduces stress, improves mood, and may have direct anticonvulsant effects through increased GABA and endorphin release. The main safety consideration is avoiding unsupervised heights or water when seizures are not fully controlled. Swimming should only be done with direct supervision. Contact sports are generally safe for patients with well-controlled epilepsy.
dailyManagement: Take antiseizure medications at the same time each day — adherence is the single most important factor in seizure control
Keep a seizure diary: record date, time, duration, description, potential triggers, and medication adherence for each seizure
Wear a medical alert bracelet or carry a wallet card with your epilepsy diagnosis and medications
Ensure adequate sleep — aim for 7-9 hours nightly and maintain consistent sleep-wake times
Communicate any seizure changes to your neurologist — new seizure types, increased frequency, or medication side effects require reassessment
Discuss contraception and pregnancy planning with your neurologist if applicable
prognosis: Prognosis varies by epilepsy type. Approximately 50-60% of people with newly diagnosed epilepsy achieve seizure freedom with the first ASM. Another 10-15% achieve seizure freedom with a second or third ASM. However, 30-40% have drug-resistant epilepsy. In drug-resistant epilepsy, the chance of achieving seizure freedom with subsequent drug trials drops to less than 5% per trial. Prognosis is better for childhood absence epilepsy, benign Rolandic epilepsy, and single-cause focal epilepsies that can be surgically treated.
recoveryTimeline: With effective ASM treatment: seizure freedom can be achieved within weeks to months of initiating the correct medication. If seizure freedom is maintained for 2-5 years (varies by epilepsy type), medication withdrawal may be considered. After epilepsy surgery: seizure freedom, if achieved, typically becomes apparent within the first 3 months.
complications: Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy (SUDEP): the leading cause of epilepsy-related death, occurring in approximately 1 in 1,000 people with epilepsy per year; risk factors include frequent generalized tonic-clonic seizures, nocturnal seizures, and poor medication adherence
Status epilepticus: life-threatening if not treated within 5 minutes; convulsive status epilepticus has a mortality of 10-20%
Injury from seizures: traumatic fractures from falls, burns, drowning, and motor vehicle accidents are significant causes of morbidity
Cognitive impairment: chronic epilepsy, particularly uncontrolled and with frequent seizures, is associated with accelerated cognitive decline
Psychiatric comorbidities: depression affects 20-35% of people with epilepsy; anxiety, psychosis, and suicide risk are significantly elevated
Antiseizure medication side effects: teratogenicity, osteoporosis, hyponatremia, weight gain, cognitive dulling — all require monitoring and management

supportResources.name: Epilepsy Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based nonprofit offering education, support services, seizure first aid training, advocacy, and a clinical trial finder for people with epilepsy.

supportResources.name: International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE)
supportResources.description: The global professional organization for epilepsy, producing the ILAE classification system, clinical guidelines, and educational resources.

supportResources.name: NICE Guideline CG137: Epilepsy in Adults. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2024.
supportResources.description: The UK clinical guideline for epilepsy diagnosis and management — freely accessible, evidence-based, and used internationally as a reference standard.

supportResources.name: WHO Epilepsy Report: Epilepsy: A Public Health Imperative. World Health Organization; 2019.
supportResources.description: WHO's global report on epilepsy as a public health priority.

confusedWithConditions.name: Syncope (Fainting)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Syncope involves brief loss of consciousness due to transient cerebral hypoperfusion, often preceded by lightheadedness, nausea, and pallor. Convulsive syncope can produce brief jerking movements during recovery but is not a seizure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Migraine with aura can produce visual disturbances, sensory symptoms, and even focal neurological deficits that may resemble focal aware seizures. Migraine aura typically develops over 5-20 minutes, lasts longer (20-60 minutes), and is followed by headache.

confusedWithConditions.name: Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TIA produces sudden focal neurological deficits lasting minutes to hours with complete resolution. TIA does not involve altered consciousness or convulsive movements.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psychogenic Non-Epileptic Seizures (PNES)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PNES are paroxysmal events that resemble seizures but are caused by psychological distress rather than abnormal electrical brain activity. EEG during an event shows no epileptiform activity. Approximately 20% of patients referred to epilepsy monitoring units are found to have PNES.

relatedConditions.name: Status Epilepticus
relatedConditions.relevance: The most feared acute complication of epilepsy — a medical emergency requiring immediate benzodiazepine treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Febrile Seizures
relatedConditions.relevance: Seizures triggered by fever in young children; a small proportion progress to epilepsy, particularly complex febrile seizures.

relatedConditions.name: Migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Migraine and epilepsy share genetic risk factors and can co-occur; they are the two most common primary brain disorders.

relatedConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Depression is the most common psychiatric comorbidity in epilepsy, affecting 20-35% of patients; the two conditions share bidirectional pathophysiology.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a seizure and epilepsy?
faqs.answer: A seizure is a single event — a brief episode of abnormal, excessive electrical activity in the brain. Epilepsy is a chronic condition defined by a sustained tendency to have recurrent, unprovoked seizures. Having one seizure does not mean you have epilepsy. Epilepsy is diagnosed when someone has at least two unprovoked seizures, or one unprovoked seizure with a high likelihood of recurrence.

faqs.question: What are the different types of seizures?
faqs.answer: The ILAE 2017 classification separates seizures into three onset categories: Focal onset (starting in one hemisphere), Generalized onset (involving both hemispheres from the start), and Unknown onset. Each type produces different symptoms and requires different treatment.

faqs.question: What should I do if someone is having a seizure?
faqs.answer: For a generalized tonic-clonic seizure: keep the person safe — move objects out of the way, place something soft under their head, do not put anything in their mouth, do not restrain them. Time the seizure. Once the seizure stops, place the person on their side in the recovery position. Call emergency services if the seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, the person does not recover, they have repeated seizures, or it is their first known seizure.

faqs.question: Can epilepsy be cured?
faqs.answer: Some epilepsy syndromes, particularly childhood absence epilepsy and benign Rolandic epilepsy, are self-limited and children outgrow them. In adults, certain focal epilepsies caused by identifiable lesions can be cured by surgical resection — approximately 60-80% of temporal lobe resections achieve seizure freedom. For most people, epilepsy is a chronic condition that can be well-controlled but not cured.

faqs.question: What medications treat epilepsy?
faqs.answer: Over 25 antiseizure medications are available. First-line choices for focal epilepsy include lamotrigine, levetiracetam, and carbamazepine. For generalized epilepsy, valproate, levetiracetam, and lamotrigine are preferred — carbamazepine and phenytoin should be avoided as they can worsen generalized seizures. The choice depends on seizure type, syndrome, age, comorbidities, and in women, reproductive plans.

faqs.question: What is drug-resistant epilepsy?
faqs.answer: Drug-resistant epilepsy is defined as failure of two appropriately chosen, well-tolerated antiseizure medications to achieve seizure freedom. This occurs in approximately 30% of people with epilepsy. When drug resistance develops, evaluation at a comprehensive epilepsy center is recommended to consider epilepsy surgery, neurostimulation devices, or dietary therapies.

faqs.question: Can people with epilepsy drive?
faqs.answer: Yes, most people with epilepsy can drive when seizures are controlled. Licensing regulations vary by country and US state, but most require a seizure-free period (typically 6-12 months) before driving. Patients are legally obligated to report their condition to the licensing authority and to their insurer.

faqs.question: Is epilepsy genetic?
faqs.answer: Some epilepsies are directly caused by a single gene mutation (monogenic). However, most common epilepsies are polygenic — many genes contribute small effects that interact with environmental factors. First-degree relatives of people with epilepsy have a 2-4 fold increased risk.

faqs.question: What is SUDEP and how can the risk be reduced?
faqs.answer: SUDEP (Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy) is the leading cause of epilepsy-related death, occurring when a person with epilepsy dies suddenly without an identifiable cause. Risk is highest in people with frequent generalized tonic-clonic seizures, nocturnal seizures, and poor medication adherence. Risk reduction strategies include achieving seizure freedom, nocturnal monitoring, and promoting medication adherence.

faqs.question: Can women with epilepsy have children?
faqs.answer: Yes, women with epilepsy can and do have healthy children. However, some antiseizure medications — particularly valproate — carry teratogenic risk. Women with epilepsy should consult with their neurologist and an obstetrician specializing in high-risk pregnancy before conception to optimize medication, ensure adequate folic acid supplementation, and plan for delivery at a center with obstetric and neonatal expertise.

faqs.question: What triggers seizures?
faqs.answer: Common seizure triggers include: sleep deprivation, alcohol consumption and especially alcohol withdrawal, stress and anxiety, flickering lights or specific visual patterns (photosensitivity), illness with fever, certain medications, hormonal changes in women, and missing doses of antiseizure medication. Not all triggers apply to all patients — keeping a seizure diary helps identify individual triggers.

faqs.question: How is epilepsy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Epilepsy diagnosis requires a thorough clinical history combined with an EEG (to identify epileptiform discharges and characterize seizure type) and MRI of the brain (to identify structural causes). Video-EEG monitoring is the gold standard for confirming the diagnosis and classifying the seizure type. Blood tests, lumbar puncture, and PET scanning may be used in specific situations.

metaTitle: Epilepsy: Types, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Epilepsy: a neurological disorder causing recurrent seizures. Learn about seizure types, causes, antiepileptic medications, epilepsy surgery, and living with epilepsy.

images.altText: Electroencephalogram (EEG) showing epileptiform spike-and-wave activity characteristic of epilepsy
images.caption: EEG showing generalized spike-and-wave discharges characteristic of genetic generalized epilepsy.

images.altText: Diagram of the brain lobes showing which cortical regions produce different seizure symptoms
images.caption: Seizure symptoms vary by the brain region involved — temporal, frontal, parietal, or occipital cortex.

images.altText: Clinical illustration of epilepsy neurosurgical procedure for seizure focus resection
images.caption: Epilepsy surgery involves precise localization and resection of the seizure onset zone.

NICE Guideline CG137: Epilepsy in Adults. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2024.
WHO/ILAE/IBE. Epilepsy: A Public Health Imperative. World Health Organization; 2019.
